sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 7, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
UP moved the black L-shape and charcoal L-shape up by 3 rows:
- Black L-shape: rows shifted from 15-23 to 12-20
- Charcoal L-shape: rows shifted from 15-23 to 12-20
So the controls work in 3-pixel increments. The yellow L-shape is at rows 45-53, cols 51-59. The moving shapes need to be positioned to match/overlap with it.
Now I need to understand what the goal is. The white dots form an L-shape pattern inside the black container. The charcoal shape is also L-shaped. The yellow shape is also L-shaped.
Looking at the shapes:
- Black L-shape with white dots: the white dots form an L pattern (horizontal bar of 3, vertical bar of 3 going down from the rightmost)
- Charcoal L-shape: looks like an inverted L (horizontal bar on top, vertical bar going down from the left)
- Yellow L-shape: looks like a rotated L (horizontal bar on top, vertical bar going down from the left)
The goal might be to position the moving shapes so they overlap with the yellow L-shape, or to match their patterns. Let me think about what ACTION7 or SPACE might do once shapes are aligned.
Actually, looking more carefully at the image, I notice the black L-shape and charcoal L-shape are on opposite sides of the sky blue divider. Maybe they're separate objects that move together.
Let me try to move the shapes to overlap with the yellow L-shape. The yellow L-shape is at rows 45-53, cols 51-59. Currently the black L-shape is at rows 12-20, cols 18-26. I need to move it down and right.
But wait - the charcoal L-shape is on the right side of the divider. Maybe only one of the shapes is the "player" and the other is a reference. Let me look at the image again.
From the image:
- Left of divider: black L-shape with white dots (this seems to be the "template" or "pattern")
- Right of divider: charcoal L-shape (this might be the movable piece)
- Bottom right: yellow L-shape (this might be the target)
Or maybe the black shape with white dots IS the player piece, and the charcoal shape is another reference. Let me try moving the shapes to overlap with the yellow one and see what happens.
The yellow L-shape boundary is [[45, 51], [45, 59], [47, 59], [47, 54], [48, 53], [53, 53], [53, 51]]
The charcoal L-shape boundary is [[12, 36], [12, 44], [14, 44], [14, 39], [15, 38], [20, 38], [20, 36]]
The charcoal L-shape needs to move to position 45-53, 51-59. Currently at 12-20, 36-44.
- Row diff: 45 - 12 = 33, so 11 DOWN moves (33/3 = 11)
- Col diff: 51 - 36 = 15, so 5 RIGHT moves (15/3 = 5)
But wait, both the black L-shape and charcoal L-shape move together. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, maybe the goal is to match the L-shape pattern. The white dots inside the black L-shape form a specific pattern. Maybe I need to move the charcoal L-shape to a position where it matches the pattern of the white dots.
Let me try a different approach - let me move the shapes down and right towards the yellow L-shape and see if something happens when they overlap.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move shapes towards the yellow L-shape target
# Yellow L-shape is at rows 45-53, cols 51-59
# Current charcoal L-shape at rows 12-20, cols 36-44
# Need: down by 33 rows (11 steps) and right by 15 cols (5 steps)
# Let's try a batch of moves
moves = ['DOWN']*11 + ['RIGHT']*5
action(moves)
print("Result:", last_action_result)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter moves:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 70:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
